titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Frances Anne Edgeworth, May 8 1843
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Wake Forest University, University of Tennessee, Xavier University, Texas A&M University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: Septemeber 18, 2025

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Frances Anne Edgeworth,

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
Trim Monday 8[superscript]th[/superscript]. 1843
74
My dearest mother,
I am steady and true to my
promise about the week and
remember I said I would not
pin myself to the day - There=
fore though I at first said
Thursday I now say Saturday
next if you please - the 13th -
I will be at home to dinner
- My dear you cannot come
to meet me unless you were
to ride one of the coach horses
which I think would be un-
seemly and rather uncom
fortably - you know that I
come home in your carriage
so I want only horses - If
yours are in good trim and
peculiarly engaged on Satur
day next you will be so good
as to send them to meet me at
[unclear]Mulligans[/unclear] - Harriet who is
always kinder than one can
conceive or invent, has contrived
for herself how she can give me
the pleasure of her company to
Mullingar and can return
without the chaise which carries
us there - She will never
heeding the [unclear] ignoming [/unclear] return
on a jaunt - or jaunting car
which she assures me she thinks
a delightful vehicle - See the
magic of that dearest of Faeries
Goodwill which can convert
the worst into the best! and al
ways prove herself a gainer
by the bargain
You hear I am quite well
ans happy and you shall
see the same on Saturday -
Love I will send to nobody
But will bring for everybody
on Saturday from all here
ever affectionately yours
to command but never to obey Maria E
75
I forgot my dear mother where
I said about Trysting place &
that Maria [deletion]wee [/deletion]had your
chaise here, & when I said about
the jaunt I still thought of
your meeting at Mullingar
- but I should not like to leave
her for that last 12 miles & so
will go on home with her &
return [unclear][/unclear] Coach. Or suppose -
suppose - that [underline]you [/underline]- I would
not trust her out of my hand to
any one else's - she caught cold
her last going home - could come
& take her -? - It is too much
care to suppose I [unclear] fear [/unclear] after your
generous deed before - so it may
be as aforsaid -
